case_name: Appeal of Stephen Verbeek & a. (New Hampshire Housing Appeals Board)

segments.text: This is case 2025-0239, appeal of Stephen Overbeek et al.
Good morning, Attorney Christensen. I think I just overheard your reserve two minutes.
Yes.
Please proceed.
Thank you very much. Please record my name is Jeffrey Christensen. I represent Stephen
and Nicole Overbeek. Well, on a property of a little over nine acres in Claremont,
this property was, would it undeveloped until they built a little camper on a tiny home
that they used for their own use, friends and family would come up and use it.
It was essentially a vacation.
Over the COVID pandemic, a few years later, they started renting it on Airbnb.
They did that for about 18 months. There were no complaints, no issues.
Neighbors said they didn't even know what was going on.
Eventually, the town found out and told them this isn't allowed under the variance under the ordinance.
You need a variance.
So they applied for that variance and it was denied by the ZBA.
Now, there's two issues with this case.
The first is whether or not the variance should have been required in the first place.
Now, the city of Claremont considered this a commercial use rather than a residential use.
This is in a residential district.
But under the cases that this court has issued, short-term rentals are not necessarily a commercial use.
It depends on the language of the zoning ordinance in this particular town.
Conway and Coogler worked with Stiff and Portsmouth that all said,
look, if the town zoning ordinance for the city zoning ordinance sets a duration requirement,
a limitation on what a residential use is, then this could exclude short-term rentals.
There wasn't one in Conway, there wasn't one in Portsmouth.
There isn't one in Claremont. There is no duration requirement for residential use.
They do have a category for hotels, motels, inns, cabins, and other transient lodges.
Transient lodging is not defined in the ordinance.
ZDA and city staff said, we're going to treat this as another transient lodging.
But this is a broad catch-all that has to be defined in relation to the other terms in this provision for hotels.
So how is the short-term rental not other transient lodging?
Exactly. So we've got, what is the distinguishing feature?
It's not the duration requirement. We know that from the zoning ordinance.
And the ZDA said, you can use this for your own short-term use.
You can have friends come up and use it for a weekend, family, you can let strangers from the internet use it for a weekend,
as long as money doesn't change hands.
So it's not the duration, and that's consistent with Conway and Kudrick and working staff.
It's not the money changing hands, however.
An apartment is not a commercial use. Simply renting something to somebody else doesn't make it a commercial use.
In a kilo of folks here from this court, we had a case where somebody had a camper,
but they were allowed to use for their personal use.
And this court said, if you can use it yourself, if you can let your friends use it, you can let people pay to use it.
Even in working staff enforcement, they said, this is short-term use.
Is it commercial use? It's being rented out.
It is prohibited.
That was a case where the property was being rented out for wedding parties or corporate events.
That's a very different circumstance.
That's much more similar to a hotel than somebody renting out what is essentially their vacation home.
There's a big difference between renting out your vacation home when you are not personally using it
or when your friends are using it, and running a hotel.
What about the issue of the lot line setback?
Are we talking about the boundaries between the two districts?
No, my understanding is that the camper itself was not setback enough from the side lot line.
Wasn't there part of a variance that requested relief from the lot line setback?
No, there was a variance request because this property is split between new districts.
And it was a variance to allow the cabin...
So there's a camper that is currently there, and they want to build a second cabin back in the woods, in the back.
This other transient lodging is allowed in the residential district.
It's only allowed in the AR district, the agricultural district, within 75 feet of that boundary.
They want to push it farther back because it's supposed to be a little cabin in the woods.
They wanted it to feel really remote.
And in this case, that was part of the appeal and part of the advantage of this,
because the way in the woods the neighbors would never...
So you don't disagree that they would need a variance for that?
Only if it counts as other transient lodging.
All of that depends on whether this is other transient lodging or simply vacation.
The city said if you want to build the cabin for your own use, you can build it all the way back.
The agricultural district allows residential use, and you can build that.
The only thing you can't do is let somebody else use it if they give you money.
That was the only part where they had an issue.
But even if we assume that these variances were required, you have the issue of the hardship.
Now, the one common reason for several variances all relating to the dimensional aspects of this
is because short-term rentals are allowed as other transient lodgings in this district
if you meet certain dimensional requirements that this one didn't need.
So it's dimensional restrictions that we need to vary as it's found.
And the common denominator for the denial is this property simply isn't unique.
You don't have a hardship.
The other two aspects of the hardship element, reasonable use, the CPA said was reasonable.
Whether there's a fair and substantial relationship justifying, denying this use,
they didn't find the only issue is this property unique.
And here the ZBA simply applied the incorrectly of the standard.
For a property to have special circumstances resulting in a hardship,
it simply has to be burdened differently than all of the properties in the district.
It doesn't have to be the singular, unique property.
In this case it is burdened differently than all the other properties in the district.
Even if we assume this is other transient lodgings,
it could have had a special exception if it had the size in the frontage and location.
But let's take size in the frontage.
This property is large enough to qualify for other transient lodgings.
It could need four acres, this has not, more than enough.
But it doesn't have enough frontage.
And the ZBA said, well, there are other large lodgings, so the size is not unique.
There are other lodgings with small frontage, so the frontage is not unique.
But there was no other lodgings that was both large and with small frontage.
So this use that would have been reasonable in this property,
but for the small frontage, would be allowed on those other properties.
The other properties of small frontage weren't large enough to make this use reasonable.
This is the only property where it could be reasonable,
but it's burdened because of its large shape.
It's got plenty of land in the back where you can put a little cabin in the woods.
But the frontage isn't there.
The frontage that is intended to say, you need this much space if you're going to operate a hotel,
do you really need that much frontage for a little cabin?
That's where the variance comes in.
And that's where they ZBA had their second legal errors when they said,
look, this is a small use, it's harmless.
But we can't consider the particulars of this use.
We have to treat it as if it were any other hotel, any other motel.
We can't say, yeah, you don't need that much frontage for this.
The fact that your property is this weird shape of a small entrance and then a large back land,
doesn't matter because they couldn't put a big hotel here.
But that's the exact opposite of the point of the variance.
The variance is not one size fits all.
It's this particular use on this particular property.
So there's a second issue.
Given where this property is, its characteristics,
it is more burdened by this zoning order than other lots in the area.
Lots that are large enough and have sufficient frontage.
Lots that are within 1,000 feet of the highway.
Like you might need for the traffic coming and going to a large hotel.
But this camper fits two people to cabin,
that two people are not going to have a lot of traffic.
Would anything stop them if they received the variance
and decided to remove the camper and replace it with a 5,000 square foot home?
Absolutely.
What would stop them?
The law in New Hampshire is when you apply for a variance,
you're applying for that particular use.
So this variance would have been for this camper, for this cabin.
Is it for the camper or for the use?
The use would be this camper.
A hotel, if they wanted to do that, they would have to go through them.
I'm not saying a hotel.
I'm saying a 5,000 square foot house.
Sure.
That would, if they wanted to put a 5,000 square foot house
for their own personal residence, they could do that.
That's permitted use.
If they wanted to put one there and rent it out,
that wouldn't be what they had applied for.
Now, in this case, it's hard to discuss the hypothetical,
but if that is just their house that they live in sometimes
and they rent it out,
I think that would be allowed just like on their Conway v. Kudry.
There is no durational requirement.
If you are treating it as a other transient lodging,
then we have to look at the specifics there.
But again, this property has those special characteristics.
If you just put a house on it,
what's the harm of somebody paying to live there
for a person who owns it?
What's the difference?
That's what we're doing.
Now, this went to the Housing Appeals Board.
The Housing Appeals Board appeared to agree
that there were special characteristics,
but they kind of dismissed that issue
and created a new basis for denial.
When they said that there was no rational connection
between proposed use and the provisions of the ordinance.
And this creates a new element that doesn't exist in the statute.
It reverses what exists in the statute.
The statute says that there has to be
no fair and substantial relationship
in order to get there.
It's the AGP said that there was a relationship
that really matters when you're having neutral,
harmless use on the statute.
If there's no harm, great.
That satisfies that element.
You don't have to justify why your use supports
the zoning ordinance purposes.
You don't have to justify why you deserve
to be able to use your property for your specific use.
And there's also a reasonability question here
when they say that there's no rational connection
between the special characteristics of this property,
its size, its frontage, its distance from the highway.
And the ordinance provisions that we're seeking a variance from,
like the size limitations and the frontage limitations
and how far it can be from the highway,
these are exactly related.
And the point is that these provisions
that make sense for a large hotel
with multiple units with a lot of people coming back for it
don't make sense for a small camper.
A cabin that you can be using anything,
but the same amount of traffic is going to happen either way.
The same amount of people, locals, not locals,
the only difference is whether or not somebody hands
and forbakes $20 when they go in or out.
That can't be the relevant difference
under the court's case law in Kudger Newark and Stiff.
There is a minimum size and there is a minimum frontage.
Some properties in the area needed, this one does not.
The use is reasonable.
The DBA said that is an unnecessary option.
These variants should be rented
if it was required in the first place.
Is there a reason to disallow renting out the camper
simply because it does not have enough frontage
to support a hotel?
Certainly.
The HAB's decision, the Housing Appeals Board's decision,
is also contrary to the record.
It went beyond the CBA's decision.
As I mentioned before, hardship has three elements.
The special characteristics, the reasonable use,
and the lack of the justifying relationship.
The ZBA only found that the special characteristics
were not met.
The other two were satisfied.
The Housing Appeals Board said,
maybe the special conditions are met,
but we're going to unsatisfy one of the other
elements.
We're going to change the ZBA's decision
on the experience of standard relationship.
We're looking for a new reason
to deny it
for the ZBA's reasonable support.
That's my top. Thank you.
Thank you, Attorney Christians.

initial_prompt: This is a New Hampshire Supreme Court oral argument. Preserve legal terminology, party names, docket numbers, and court names. Likely speakers include justices and counsel.
text:
This is case 2025-0239, appeal of Stephen Overbeek et al.

Good morning, Attorney Christensen. I think I just overheard your reserve two minutes.

Yes.

Please proceed.

Thank you very much. Please record my name is Jeffrey Christensen. I represent Stephen

and Nicole Overbeek. Well, on a property of a little over nine acres in Claremont,

this property was, would it undeveloped until they built a little camper on a tiny home

that they used for their own use, friends and family would come up and use it.

It was essentially a vacation.

Over the COVID pandemic, a few years later, they started renting it on Airbnb.

They did that for about 18 months. There were no complaints, no issues.

Neighbors said they didn't even know what was going on.

Eventually, the town found out and told them this isn't allowed under the variance under the ordinance.

You need a variance.

So they applied for that variance and it was denied by the ZBA.

Now, there's two issues with this case.

The first is whether or not the variance should have been required in the first place.

Now, the city of Claremont considered this a commercial use rather than a residential use.

This is in a residential district.

But under the cases that this court has issued, short-term rentals are not necessarily a commercial use.

It depends on the language of the zoning ordinance in this particular town.

Conway and Coogler worked with Stiff and Portsmouth that all said,

look, if the town zoning ordinance for the city zoning ordinance sets a duration requirement,

a limitation on what a residential use is, then this could exclude short-term rentals.

There wasn't one in Conway, there wasn't one in Portsmouth.

There isn't one in Claremont. There is no duration requirement for residential use.

They do have a category for hotels, motels, inns, cabins, and other transient lodges.

Transient lodging is not defined in the ordinance.

ZDA and city staff said, we're going to treat this as another transient lodging.

But this is a broad catch-all that has to be defined in relation to the other terms in this provision for hotels.

So how is the short-term rental not other transient lodging?

Exactly. So we've got, what is the distinguishing feature?

It's not the duration requirement. We know that from the zoning ordinance.

And the ZDA said, you can use this for your own short-term use.

You can have friends come up and use it for a weekend, family, you can let strangers from the internet use it for a weekend,

as long as money doesn't change hands.

So it's not the duration, and that's consistent with Conway and Kudrick and working staff.

It's not the money changing hands, however.

An apartment is not a commercial use. Simply renting something to somebody else doesn't make it a commercial use.

In a kilo of folks here from this court, we had a case where somebody had a camper,

but they were allowed to use for their personal use.

And this court said, if you can use it yourself, if you can let your friends use it, you can let people pay to use it.

Even in working staff enforcement, they said, this is short-term use.

Is it commercial use? It's being rented out.

It is prohibited.

That was a case where the property was being rented out for wedding parties or corporate events.

That's a very different circumstance.

That's much more similar to a hotel than somebody renting out what is essentially their vacation home.

There's a big difference between renting out your vacation home when you are not personally using it

or when your friends are using it, and running a hotel.

What about the issue of the lot line setback?

Are we talking about the boundaries between the two districts?

No, my understanding is that the camper itself was not setback enough from the side lot line.

Wasn't there part of a variance that requested relief from the lot line setback?

No, there was a variance request because this property is split between new districts.

And it was a variance to allow the cabin...

So there's a camper that is currently there, and they want to build a second cabin back in the woods, in the back.

This other transient lodging is allowed in the residential district.

It's only allowed in the AR district, the agricultural district, within 75 feet of that boundary.

They want to push it farther back because it's supposed to be a little cabin in the woods.

They wanted it to feel really remote.

And in this case, that was part of the appeal and part of the advantage of this,

because the way in the woods the neighbors would never...

So you don't disagree that they would need a variance for that?

Only if it counts as other transient lodging.

All of that depends on whether this is other transient lodging or simply vacation.

The city said if you want to build the cabin for your own use, you can build it all the way back.

The agricultural district allows residential use, and you can build that.

The only thing you can't do is let somebody else use it if they give you money.

That was the only part where they had an issue.

But even if we assume that these variances were required, you have the issue of the hardship.

Now, the one common reason for several variances all relating to the dimensional aspects of this

is because short-term rentals are allowed as other transient lodgings in this district

if you meet certain dimensional requirements that this one didn't need.

So it's dimensional restrictions that we need to vary as it's found.

And the common denominator for the denial is this property simply isn't unique.

You don't have a hardship.

The other two aspects of the hardship element, reasonable use, the CPA said was reasonable.

Whether there's a fair and substantial relationship justifying, denying this use,

they didn't find the only issue is this property unique.

And here the ZBA simply applied the incorrectly of the standard.

For a property to have special circumstances resulting in a hardship,

it simply has to be burdened differently than all of the properties in the district.

It doesn't have to be the singular, unique property.

In this case it is burdened differently than all the other properties in the district.

Even if we assume this is other transient lodgings,

it could have had a special exception if it had the size in the frontage and location.
